title: Rādhākṛṣṇāṣṭakālīyalīlāsmaraṇakramapaddhati
author: Dhyānacandra Gosvāmī
summary: A Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual outlining the systematic sequence (krama) for the meditative remembrance (smaraṇa) of the aṣṭakālīya-līlā, the eight daily periods of the eternal pastimes of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa in Vraja. It guides the practitioner through visualizing and mentally serving the divine couple at each part of the day, cultivating mañjarī-bhāva (the mood of intimate female attendants). It forms part of the larger Gaura-Govindārcana-smaraṇa-paddhati tradition of esoteric worship.
history: Dhyānacandra was a disciple of Gopālaguru Gosvāmī, and composed his paddhati following the format established by his guru; together their two same-titled manuals are the earliest and foundational handbooks of Gauḍīya esoteric (rāgānugā) deity worship and smaraṇa. The text is transmitted within the Caitanya tradition lineage at Purī and Vṛndāvana.

title: Harināmacintāmaṇi
author: Kedāranātha Datta (Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura)
genre: philosophical/devotional treatise on nāma (the holy name)
summary: A treatise expounding the philosophy and practice of harināma, the chanting of Kṛṣṇa's holy names, cast as a dialogue between Caitanya Mahāprabhu and Haridāsa Ṭhākura. It systematically analyzes the offenses against the holy name (nāmāparādha), the gradations of chanting, and the path from nāmābhāsa (a semblance of the name) to pure nāma, culminating in love of God.
history: Bhaktivinoda Ṭhākura (Kedarnath Datta, 1838–1914) was a Bengali magistrate, philosopher and reformer who revitalized Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism; from 1874 until his death he wrote numerous works in Sanskrit, Bengali and English. The Harināmacintāmaṇi is among his influential expository works, widely studied in the Gauḍīya line that produced the later Gauḍīya Maṭha and ISKCON movements.

title: Haribhaktivilāsa 1–11
author: Gopāla Bhaṭṭa Gosvāmī (largely composed/redacted by Sanātana Gosvāmī)
summary: An extensive Sanskrit ritual compendium describing the normative ritual and devotional life of a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava: initiation (dīkṣā), daily worship, mantras, vows, festivals, deity service, and conduct. The work is largely a digest, with over 90% of its verses cited from earlier Vedic, Tantric, Pāñcarātra and Purāṇic sources. This entry covers the first eleven of the twenty vilāsas (sections).
history: Traditionally attributed to Gopāla Bhaṭṭa, one of the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana, but the Bhaktiratnākara (1.197–198) records that the idea originated with Gopāla Bhaṭṭa while Sanātana Gosvāmī actually carried out the composition in his name. It became the foundational ritual authority for the entire Gauḍīya tradition and is accompanied by the Dig-darśinī commentary attributed to Sanātana.

title: Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
summary: A short theological dissertation (c. 150 verses) establishing the rationale for worshipping Rādhā together with Kṛṣṇa. Arguing first that devotees are to be worshipped after the Lord, Jīva grades the devotees and demonstrates with abundant scriptural citation that the gopīs of Vṛndāvana, and Rādhā above all, are the topmost devotees and thus worthy of worship alongside Kṛṣṇa.
history: Composed by Jīva Gosvāmī (c. 1513–1598), nephew of Rūpa and Sanātana and the great systematizer of Gauḍīya theology. It articulates the doctrinal basis for the distinctive Gauḍīya worship of the divine couple Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa and remains a standard reference in the tradition's arcana literature.

title: Jñānāmṛtasāra-Saṃhitā [Nārada-Pañcarātra]
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: late saṃhitā stratum, probably c. 12th–16th c. CE
summary: A Pāñcarātra saṃhitā of just over 3100 ślokas in sixty chapters (in five sections), narrated by Vyāsa as a revelation given by Maheśvara (Śiva) to Nārada. It glorifies the cult of Kṛṣṇa, Gopāla and Rādhā, and expounds five kinds of knowledge—of the Absolute, liberation, devotional service, yoga, and ignorance—with devotion to Kṛṣṇa as its leading theme. It is popularly circulated under the title Nārada-Pañcarātra.
history: Reckoned among the latest of the Pāñcarātra saṃhitās, it shows the school's evolution toward Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa sectarian devotion: older Pāñcarātra concepts such as the vyūhas recede while śakti as creative power gains prominence. It was popular in Bengal and influenced Gauḍīya circles, where it is frequently cited.

title: Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha
author: Siddha Kṛṣṇadāsa Tātapāda (Govardhana-nivāsī Kṛṣṇadāsa)
genre: compilation / meditation (smaraṇa) manual for rāgānugā bhakti
summary: A compiled handbook on the inner meditative practice (bhāvanā, smaraṇa) of rāgānugā bhakti, gathering material on cultivating the siddha-deha (the meditator's perfected spiritual body) and on mentally serving Rādhā-Kṛṣṇa in Vraja day and night. It belongs to the esoteric Gauḍīya literature on the eightfold daily pastimes and internal devotional absorption.
history: Compiled by Kṛṣṇadāsa Tātapāda, a siddha resident of Govardhana, within the Gauḍīya rāgānugā tradition of meditative worship. It survives in manuscript and printed (Hindi-translated) editions and is part of the practical literature on siddha-praṇālī and mañjarī-bhāva.

title: Kramadīpikā
author: Keśava Kāśmīrī (Keśava Bhaṭṭa)
summary: A work in eight chapters dealing chiefly with the ritualistic side of Vaiṣṇava worship, devoted largely to the various mantras (especially the ten- and eighteen-syllable Gopāla mantras), their nyāsa and meditation, and associated procedures of Kṛṣṇa worship. It was influential in temple ritual, including at the Jagannātha temple in Purī.
history: Authored by Keśava Kāśmīrī (Keśava Bhaṭṭācārya), an ācārya of the Nimbārka Sampradāya, who names himself in the colophon. The text was widely respected across Vaiṣṇava lineages and is quoted in the Gauḍīya Haribhaktivilāsa, attesting to its authority in mantra and ritual matters.

title: Mukundamālā
author: Kulaśekhara (Kulaśekhara Āḻvār)
summary: A Sanskrit devotional hymn of about 40 verses addressed to Kṛṣṇa/Viṣṇu as Mukunda, the bestower of liberation. The verses express ardent surrender (prapatti) and longing for the Lord, including the famous prayer to remember Kṛṣṇa at the moment of death, and extol single-minded devotion over even liberation.
history: Attributed to Kulaśekhara, the poet-saint counted among the twelve Āḻvārs of the Tamil Śrī Vaiṣṇava tradition, identified with a Cera (Kulaśekhara) ruler of Kerala; the hymn is traditionally associated with the Raṅganātha temple at Śrīraṅgam. Its authorship and date are debated, but it is a celebrated and widely commented Vaiṣṇava stotra. The project hint of c. 900–1099 CE is broadly consistent with common datings.

title: Laghubhāgavatāmṛta
author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
summary: A theological treatise on Kṛṣṇa-tattva and avatāra-tattva that establishes Kṛṣṇa as the avatārī—the original Supreme source of all incarnations—rather than one incarnation among many. It classifies the forms of Godhead (svayaṃ-rūpa, tadekātma-rūpa with its vilāsa and svāṃśa subdivisions, and āveśa forms) and the categories of devotees, arguing for the supremacy of Kṛṣṇa over Nārāyaṇa and other manifestations.
history: Composed by Rūpa Gosvāmī (c. 1489–1564), the foremost theologian among the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana, as a counterpart to the larger Bṛhad-bhāgavatāmṛta of his brother (here the longer work is by Sanātana). It is a core doctrinal statement of Gauḍīya Kṛṣṇa-centric theology.

title: Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava
author: Madhva (Ānandatīrtha / Pūrṇaprajña)
summary: "The Great Ocean of the Nectar of Kṛṣṇa," a devotional work of about 242–290 verses praising Kṛṣṇa's divine attributes and the fruits of devoted worship—asserting, for instance, that one who worships him with full devotion is never condemned. It compiles scriptural verses and instruction on the merits of Kṛṣṇa-bhakti, including ethical teachings (e.g., the qualities that make a person worthy of respect).
history: One of the thirty-seven works (sarvamūla granthas) of Madhvācārya (Ānandatīrtha), founder of the Dvaita school of Vedānta. It frames Kṛṣṇa-devotion within his realist, dualist theology and is composed, in his words, to give nectar to those poisoned by saṃsāra.

title: Muktācarita
author: Raghunāthadāsa Gosvāmī
summary: A short allegorical narrative ("The Story of the Pearl") set in the divine pastimes of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa in Vraja, in which a playful dispute over pearls becomes a vehicle for devotional and theological themes. It is one of the small esoteric compositions illustrating the intimate līlā of the divine couple.
history: One of three works by Raghunāthadāsa Gosvāmī (c. 1495–1571), one of the Six Gosvāmīs of Vṛndāvana renowned for his renunciation and his poetry of separation (vipralambha). His works, including the Dānakelicintāmaṇi and Stavāvalī, are treasured in the Gauḍīya tradition for their depiction of mañjarī-bhāva.

title: Prakāśa-Saṃhitā [Pāñcarātra-Saṃhitā]
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A Pāñcarātra saṃhitā, one of the numerous Vaiṣṇava āgamic texts of that canon concerned with the four standard divisions of Pāñcarātra subject matter—jñāna (theology/cosmology), yoga, kriyā (temple construction and image-making), and caryā (worship and conduct). Specific contents are poorly documented in accessible scholarship.
history: Named in traditional lists of Pāñcarātra saṃhitās but little studied; it belongs to the broad body of more than two hundred Pāñcarātra texts that shaped Vaiṣṇava temple ritual and theology. Its date and provenance are not securely established.

title: Pauṣkara-Saṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: early/ancient Pāñcarātra stratum, c. 5th–8th c. CE (disputed)
summary: One of the most ancient and respected Pāñcarātra saṃhitās, a large work (roughly 5900 ślokas in 43 chapters in its published form) framed as a dialogue between the sage Pauṣkara and Bhagavān, covering the full range of Pāñcarātra concern—jñāna, yoga, kriyā and caryā. This entry covers chapters 27–43, dealing with the later ritual portions (temple/image and worship procedures). It is especially esteemed by the temple priests of Śrīraṅgam and Kāñcīpuram.
history: Together with the Sātvata and Jayākhya saṃhitās it forms the ratnatraya ("three jewels"), the most authoritative and earliest core of the Pāñcarātra canon. Its antiquity and continued liturgical use give it foundational status in Śrī Vaiṣṇava temple worship.

title: Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu
author: Rūpa Gosvāmī
summary: The foundational systematic treatise on bhakti-rasa, adapting the classical rasa theory of Bharata's Nāṭyaśāstra into a theology of devotion. In four divisions (eastern, southern, western, northern "quadrants"), it analyzes the stages of devotional practice (sādhana, bhāva, prema), the constituents of devotional emotion (vibhāva, anubhāva, sāttvika-bhāva, vyabhicāri-bhāva), and the five primary and seven secondary rasas of relationship with Kṛṣṇa.
history: Composed by Rūpa Gosvāmī in 1541 CE (1463 Śaka), it is the central theoretical work of Gauḍīya devotional aesthetics, transforming bhakti into a fully theorized rasa. It is paired with its sequel on the erotic mode, the Ujjvalanīlamaṇi, and was translated and studied extensively by David Haberman and others.

title: Sādhanādīpikā
author: Rādhākṛṣṇadāsa Gosvāmī
summary: A Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual on devotional practice (sādhana), treating deity worship (vigraha-sevā / arcana), daily ritual, and the broader regimen of bhakti. It includes discussion of the authorization of deity service in the tradition (e.g., Mahāprabhu's empowering of Gadādhara Paṇḍita for vigraha-sevā) alongside practical worship procedures.
history: Authored by Rādhākṛṣṇadāsa Gosvāmī within the Gauḍīya line; the work is cited in discussions of the tradition's standards of deity worship and Gadādhara's role in the sampradāya. Precise biographical and dating details for the author are not well established in accessible scholarship.

title: Sātvatatantra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A Pāñcarātra text (sātvata-śāstra) presented as taught by Nārada and propounded by Viśoka, offering a path to liberation (mukti) made accessible even to women, śūdras and the low-born. "Sātvata" refers to an archaic designation of the ancient Bhāgavata/Sātvata bhakti cult, and the text treats Vaiṣṇava theology and devotional means within that framework.
history: Distinguished in some catalogues from the famous Sātvata Saṃhitā (one of the ratnatraya): wisdomlib treats the Sātvatatantra as a separate text attributed to Viśoka. It is frequently cited in later Vaiṣṇava, including Gauḍīya, literature. Its precise identity, dating and relation to the Sātvata Saṃhitā are not fully settled.

title: Daśavidhahetunirūpaṇa
author: Śrīnivāsamakhin (Śrīnivāsa Dīkṣita) Vedāntadeśika
summary: A doctrinal treatise setting out ten arguments (daśavidha-hetu) for the superiority of the Vaikhānasa school among the Vaiṣṇava sects, serving as an introduction to the larger Vaikhānasasūtratātparyacintāmaṇi. It defends the authority and distinctive practices of the Vaikhānasa tradition of temple worship.
history: Despite being grouped with Gauḍīya/Vaiṣṇava e-texts (GRETIL), this is a Vaikhānasa apologetic work by Śrīnivāsamakhin ("Vedāntadeśika" here being an epithet, not the famous Śrī Vaiṣṇava Vedānta Deśika). It is transmitted with Sanskrit and Telugu commentaries and is part of the Vaikhānasa sectarian literature.

title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 1. Tattvasandarbha
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
summary: The first of the six Sandarbhas, establishing the epistemological foundation of Gauḍīya theology: it examines the means of valid knowledge (pramāṇa), argues that scripture (śabda) and especially the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is the supreme authority on the Absolute, and identifies the Bhāgavata as both the highest means of knowing and the ultimate object of knowledge.
history: Jīva Gosvāmī (c. 1513–1598) systematized into the Ṣaṭsandarbha ideas inherited from Rūpa, Sanātana, and (per tradition) preliminary notes of Gopāla Bhaṭṭa. The Tattvasandarbha is the prolegomenon to the whole corpus, providing the methodological basis for the Gauḍīya Acintya-bhedābheda Vedānta; it carries commentaries by Jīva himself and later by Baladeva Vidyābhūṣaṇa.

title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 2. Bhāgavatasandarbha
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
summary: The second of the six Sandarbhas (also called Bhagavat-sandarbha), expounding the ontology of the non-dual Absolute Reality and establishing Bhagavān—the personal Lord endowed with all energies—as its fullest and highest expression, above the impersonal Brahman and the inner-controller Paramātmā. It develops the doctrine of the Lord's intrinsic potencies (śaktis).
history: Part of Jīva Gosvāmī's systematic theological corpus arranging the teachings of the early Gauḍīya masters, addressing the theme of sambandha (the ontological relation between God, his energies, and souls). With the next two Sandarbhas it forms the bhagavat-tattva exposition of the school.

title: Ṣaṭsandarbha: 3. Paramātmasandarbha
author: Jīva Gosvāmī
summary: The third of the six Sandarbhas, examining the nature of the individual self (jīva), the operation of māyā, and the immanent presence of the Absolute as Paramātmā (the Supersoul) pervading and sustaining the cosmos and all beings. It elaborates the relation between the soul, the material energy, and the indwelling Lord.
history: Continuing Jīva Gosvāmī's systematic Bhāgavata Vedānta, it treats the theme of sambandha alongside the Tattva- and Bhāgavata-sandarbhas, working out the metaphysics of jīva and māyā that underpins the school's acintya-bhedābheda (inconceivable difference-and-non-difference) doctrine.
